content:
3 min read Just now -- I thought the React app was fine. It had Redux, reusable components, clean folder structure, and proper API integration. But once it hit production… users started complaining. Pages were slow. Interactions lagged. The UI felt “heavy”. That’s when I realized something important: React apps don’t fail in development — they fail in production scale. Here’s exactly what was wrong and what actually fixed it. 1. Problem: The App Was Re-rendering Too Much The first issue was not obvious at all. A simple state update was triggering multiple unnecessary re-renders across the app. Example of the issue: const Parent = () => { const [count, setCount] = useState(0); return ( <> ); };Even when Child had nothing to do with count, it kept re-rendering. Fix: React.memo + proper component isolation const Child = React.memo(() => { console.log("Child rendered"); return

Hello

; });Result: • Reduced unnecessary renders • Improved UI responsiveness • Clean separation of concerns 2. Problem: API Calls Were Triggering Too Often One of the worst performance issues was repeated API calls. Example: useEffect(() => { fetchUsers(); });This caused API spam on every render. Fix: Proper dependency management useEffect(() => { fetchUsers(); }, []);Even better: • Introduced caching layer • Used request deduplication • Added loading state control Result: • 60% reduction in API calls • Faster UI rendering 3. Problem: Huge Bundle Size The app was loading everything at once. Users were waiting too long on initial load. Fix: Lazy Loading + Code Splitting const Dashboard = React.lazy(() => import("./Dashboard")); Loading...}> Result: • Faster first paint • Improved Lighthouse score • Better user experience 4. Problem: Bad State Management Strategy We made a classic mistake: Putting too much in global Redux state. Everything was global: • UI state • API state • temporary values This caused unnecessary global updates. Fix: Split state properly Rule we adopted: • Local state → UI-specific data • Global state → shared data only const [modalOpen, setModalOpen] = useState(false);Result: • Cleaner architecture • Reduced re-renders • Easier debugging 5. Problem: Rendering Large Lists One screen had thousands of rows. The UI almost froze. Fix: Virtualization import { FixedSizeList as List } from "react-window";Result: • Only visible items render • Smooth scrolling • Massive performance improvement 6. The Real Root Cause (Most Important Insight) After fixing everything, I realized something surprising: The biggest issue wasn’t React itself — it was architecture decisions made too early. We optimized components, but the real improvement came from: • Better separation of logic • Better state design • Better rendering strategy • Better API structure Final Results After Optimization After all fixes: • Page load speed improved significantly • API calls reduced drastically • UI became smooth and responsive • Developer experience improved too Final Thoughts React performance issues are rarely caused by one big mistake. They are usually caused by many small architectural decisions that compound over time. If you’re building React apps, focus on: • Component design • State strategy • Rendering optimization • API structure Because in real production apps: Clean architecture matters more than clever code. Have you ever faced a React app that worked fine in development but broke in production performance? What was the root cause?
